enough patience you will always manage to get adhesions down so this is us doing the densest part of the adhesions on the thoracotomy really just burning right onto the ribs and even just a few of these little parenchymal injuries they do tend to heal with a couple of days with a drain in you can use some glue but we didn't in this case so that was the thoracotomy adhesions all taken down and then we

CO2 is absolutely vital for diaphragm placation and so it was really important that we had to go in and have good CO2 seals in the chest.

and we're just assessing to see where to put our horizontal mattress sutures.
If you haven't seen it before, this is the EndoStitch by Cavidian, absolutely fantastic device, totally vital for VATS diaphragm placation.
It's the second thing that makes these cases really easy.
The first thing is CO2, cranked up to a nice high pressure together with the patient at about 30 degrees, head up, and then the second thing is the EndoStitch.
So we do a long line of horizontal, plegeted mattress sutures.
We use zero non-absorbable braided suture.
And because we do multiple horizontal mattresses, even if one were to fail, the other sort of eight or nine would still hold.
Therefore not being reliant on a single suture.
So we tend to suture anterolateral to posteromedial in a long line, trying to have that line over the apex of the paralyzed diaphragm.
So hopefully it gives a good long term result.
This is going towards the medial side of the placation.
And then the other thing to note, of course, is that we grab the diaphragm with a Maryland and we make sure there's absolutely no bowel in it.
Because if we grabbed it and it's nice and narrow like that, you can't possibly grab a piece of bowel.
And then we just suture just round our Maryland and place all these sutures.
I do routinely put an NG tube down for left-sided placations just to further compress the stomach and get it out the way.
And now you can see we're now doing the slightly more lateral horizontal mattress sutures, again doubling over the diaphragm.
You can see because I'm pulling it over itself again, that's another good safety step so that you don't cause any damage to the structures below.
We have a nice knot pusher and endoscopic scissors.
Of note, we put all the pledges on outside of the chest, which makes them very straightforward to place.
This operation does cause some pleural effusions because it's still a paralysed diaphragm, and so I leave a drain in.
for about two or three days post-operatively to try and reduce the chance of there being a significant post-operative pleural effusion.

And it's actually quite easy to find the intercostal nerves because you just feel the rib and go just below it.

It's meant to preserve the swan sheath and only damage the axons for a couple of months, and then the nerve is meant to successfully grow back after that.
So in my short experience, this does give two to three months of good analgesia.
